title: Log cabin weave on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: A two-colour block pattern woven at 50/50 warp and weft ratio on a single heddle
excerpt: Log cabin is a classic colour-block weave structure produced from a two-colour warp and a two-colour weft. Alternating the warp colour in adjacent threads and alternating the weft colour between picks produces a grid of interlocking colour blocks. The structure works on a single rigid heddle without pickup sticks, provided the threading is set up correctly at warping time.
sourceNotes: Log cabin weave structure is a documented plain-weave variation used in American and Scandinavian textile traditions. Technique is observation-led.

content.text: warp alternates two colours: light/dark/light/dark across every thread position. This is set up at the warping board, not at the heddle. The
in the finished cloth form by switching weft colour after a set number of picks: 4 picks of light, then 4 picks of dark, and so on.
This pattern produces a 25 cm x 30 cm swatch at 8 EPI. Scale warp length and width for larger projects. Both warp and weft use the same yarn weight.

items.name: DK cotton or wool yarn, colour A (light)
DK cotton or wool yarn, colour B (dark)
Rigid heddle loom, 8-dent reed, minimum 30 cm width
Two stick shuttles (one per weft colour)

content.text: Wind the warp alternating colour A and colour B with every thread. The sequence must be strict: A, B, A, B throughout. Do not group two A threads or two B threads together: this breaks the log cabin structure. For a 25-cm-wide warp at 8 EPI, wind 20 threads of each colour (40 total).
Step 2: Thread the heddle in alternating colour order

Thread the heddle maintaining the A/B alternation from the warp. Colour A threads go through the holes; colour B threads go through the slots (or vice versa: the important thing is consistency, not which is which). This threading ensures each colour surfaces on opposite faces of the cloth.

Load shuttle 1 with colour A and shuttle 2 with colour B. Weave 4 picks of colour A (alternating up-shed and down-shed). Switch to colour B and weave 4 picks. The pattern blocks will appear as colour A squares alternating with colour B squares in a grid. Adjust block size by changing the number of picks per colour.

attrs.intro: One common warping error that prevents log cabin from forming.

items.fix: Check the warp at the cross. Each pair of threads at the cross should be colour A then colour B. If two same-colour threads are adjacent, re-thread those positions before weaving further.
items.cause: The warp was not wound in strict alternating colours, or two threads of the same colour were threaded adjacent to each other.
items.symptom: No colour blocks visible. The cloth looks like plain weave in a single colour

attrs.heading: Common problems
